Yes, it is possible to differentiate between running a Flask app as a server and running a CLI command, and you can even pass parameters to `create_app` when running the CLI command without the need for an environment variable. This can be achieved by using the `@app.cli.command` decorator and leveraging the context of how the Flask app is being used. When running the app using `flask run`, the app is initialized using the default `create_app` function, while when running a CLI command like `flask my_command`, the same `create_app` function is used, but the CLI command has access to the Flask app instance. This means you can modify the behavior of `create_app` based on whether the app is being used as a server or as a CLI command. 

To do this, you can use the `flask` command-line interface to run your CLI commands, and you can pass parameters to the `create_app` function by using the `@app.cli.command` decorator and the `params` argument. For example, you can define a CLI command that takes a parameter and use that parameter to customize the `create_app` function. This approach allows you to run `flask my_command` with different configurations without having to set an environment variable. 

You can also use the `flask` command-line interface to run your CLI commands and pass parameters to the `create_app` function by using the `@app.cli.command` decorator and the `params` argument. This approach allows you to run `flask my_command` with different configurations without having to set an environment variable.
